speaker: interviewer
text: Louis Goldman, interviewer James Alexander Bond, New York City, USA, English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was fortunate to find this couple, man and wife, who operated a portrait studio in Montpelier called Studio Harry, H-A-R-R-Y. And I was hidden there in the darkroom in the back. And I lived there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You must realize that this already was a great risk to the people hiding me. But living there, I started to pick up basic elements of photography. And I spent my time, ironically, in the darkroom in the back developing pictures of German soldiers, who had come in to have their portraits taken to send to their wives, or sweethearts, or whatever. I stayed there for quite a while [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until a new law came out saying everybody needs new identification papers, new French identification papers. And they thought that this would be a good opportunity for, for me to acquire one. I had a-- I had a false one on the name of Paul Boreel, B-O-R-E-E-L, born in some French town and so forth. But it was a fake one. The new law said you have to turn in the fake one. But you have to turn in the previous one and get a new one. So we travelled to a town. The husband came with me because he needed one, one, too. He was born in England. And that didn't go over very well. So he figured he, too, was going to ask for a new one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we both went to a town. And you must realize that we took a tremendous chance. Because if they discovered that our cards were fake, we would immediately be arrested. But you had to, because the old ones were no longer valid. You had to show the new one now. So we went with palpitating hearts to the police station. We turned in our old cards. And they said, come back in the afternoon. Your cards will be ready. So we spent five agonizing hours wandering around the city, wondering whether it's wise at all to go back. But we figured we would risk it. We went, and believe it or not, our cards were ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. And we were out of there, feeling suddenly like having been granted permission to, to live. We were now French with the new cards. Nothing could stop us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's when I decided having this card now, being a legitimate French citizen-- non-Jew, because Jews had a large J on their cards for Juif, which is French for Jew-- I now decided it's safe enough to go and visit my family in the Italian zone. They been designated. You couldn't just live anywhere that you wanted in the Italian zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Italian authorities had designated various areas as residence forcee, which means you had to live in that area. You could only leave in an emergency, medical and so forth. Otherwise, you had to register, I believe, twice a week at the local Italian command post. But beyond that, in the Italian zone, there was absolutely no fear of deportation, of arrests, or persecution, beating, and so forth, which was kind of as close as you could come at that time to normal living. I went to visit my family in that Italian zone. And they lived-- they had been designated to live in a town called Saint-Martin-Vesubie, which is about three-hour bus ride up into the mountains above Nice. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got to the bus station. There were only two buses a day to that village, small town. When I got to the bus station, it was full. And I didn't look forward to three hours standing all the way up there. I figured I'll take the, the afternoon bus. So I went to the hotel where I had stayed overnight. I went back and went back to my room. And a few minutes later there was a knock on the door, and two French inspectors were there. Papers, please. So I showed them my papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, something tells me that you're Jewish. I said, me? Jewish? Ha. Da, da, dee. Da, da, da. He said, well, let's examine this. So he sat down and started asking me questions, which I answered and so forth. At the end, he said, look. We're not after Jews. We're after black market dealers. And if you will come with us to the police station, we can clarify this. So I saw-- I saw myself trapped already. I could not say, no. I don't want to go to the police station. I had to go with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And arriving at the station, one of them started filling out a whole protocol, typing the papers. The other one had said nothing all this, this time. He was just standing there. The one that was typing up the report had to leave. He was called away. So the other one said, you fool. You idiot, he said. If you had maintained, and been a little more aggressive, we would not have taken you. He said, now it's too late. I can't help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he put me-- they put me in a jail cell together with the pimps, and the thieves, and the black marketeers, and so forth. And in the afternoon, I heard my name called. And the gendarme came and took me to the office of the commander of that prison. And sitting there was a man whom I did not, not know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- The man in charge said, if you answer the right questions, I can let you go. So I said, what, what are the, what are the questions? He said, what are you dealing in? I said, I'm not dealing in anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, we suspect you of being in the black market. You must know that at the time food was strictly rationed. You only got a specific quantity of bread, butter, oil, sugar, whatever a month. But there was a very lively, active black market going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I convinced him that I had nothing to do with a black, black market. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said, well, it's your lucky day. I entrust you to this, this man. You may go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man took me, and we walked out. And I was still dizzy, not comprehending what had happened. It transpires that he was a French Jew highly decorated in the first World War. And he was instrumental in helping the police catch a band of thieves who were burglarizing the villas along the Riviera. And in exchange, he said, I hear that you caught a Jewish boy this morning. I want him in exchange. So I was free, but not as free as I was before. They had taken away my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] official fake French card. I was now allowed to remain in Nice. But I had to report to the police, I forgot, once a week or more. This didn't last long. I stayed in Nice. I even found a little job in a-- in a photo studio there, again, darkroom, until September 8, 1943. On that day, Italy capitulated. Marshal Badoglio asked for an armistice. He was the supreme command of the Italian forces-- asked for an armistice, which was signed immediately. And all the Italian troops in the French zone got orders to retreat immediately into Italy. Now, we knew what was going to happen. The Germans were going to come and occupy Nice and the former Italian zone immediately within hours. What followed was a mass exodus of thousands of Jewish refugees, who had, until then, found refuge in the Italian zone into Italy, thinking that arriving in, in Italy proper, the war would be over since Italy capitulated, was now going to be occupied by the Allied, who, until then, were bogged down in the south of Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had landed in Anzio. They had landed in Sicily, and then, of course, had a toehold in Italy proper, but way down south. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened was exactly the contrary. We crossed the Alps on foot from Saint-Martin into Italy-- no small undertaking for people who are not mountain climbers, who had just the shirts on their backs and perhaps some pathetic little bit of luggage climbing over the, the, the Alps. But most of our community in Saint-Martin-- Oh, how did I know about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Nice. And I received a telegram from my mother to come and join them immediately, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I did. And then my family had already left. But they told us-- they left a note saying where I should meet them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's where I joined them. OK. We're now in, in Italy. And we're inquiring, where are the nearest American British soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, they said. The Germans are, are coming in, pouring in, and are taking over, which they did, in, in, in fact. In a matter of days. they had taken over every inch of Italian territory, which was now under German control.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had no choice but to remain up in the mountains in, in Italy. Now, you can imagine, we had no Italian money, we had no Italian papers, we had no Italian Russian cards, we didn't speak the language, and we had nowhere to, to stay. So we, we remained in the mountains, sleeping in the woods or in abandoned cow sheds and, and, and so forth all through September and October, at which time a man came to see us, which we later learned his name was Rafael Cantoni. And he was dispatched by the Florence Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now remember, where we were is right across the French border, way up there in Northwest Italy. Florence is halfway down the Italian peninsula. He came from Florence on behalf of the Jewish community there, because they had heard of that mass influx of Jewish refugees into Italy, who had nowhere to go and no means of surviving, and winter was coming-- It was getting very cold, and we were not equipped in any way-- saying that if we want to take the chance and travel from where we were all the way to Florence, if we make it, there's a committee there composed of the Jewish community and of Italian Catholic priests, who would help us in hiding, finding hiding, hiding places. The decision was up to us. We decided we would go, like most people did. Very few remained on the mountains because the outlook was grim. The war was nowhere near ending. We lived mostly on various concoction made of chestnuts, which grew abundantly in those areas. We cooked them, we broiled them, we chopped them. We did everything that was possible. We decided we would go. We went. Our group was about eight, eight people, our family, three, and some others. We traveled by train from where we were to Cuneo, from Cuneo to Torino, Turin, continuing all the time to what's Florence. Somewhere between Genova and Florence, the train was suddenly stopped. We heard sirens. And the train was suddenly stopped. We looked outside. We were in the middle of nowhere. There was no railroad station near us, nothing that indicated or justified a stop. I looked out the windows, and I immediately saw that the entire train was surrounded by German soldiers with their arms at the ready, with their guns at, at the ready. We knew that this is the end of the line. They were looking for Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in effect, we saw, coming down one railroad car after another, a German officer accompanied by two [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fascist guards, Italian, and an interpreter. And they picked people, here and there. Show us document, document. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they came to us, a comedy kind of started. We had no, no papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father took out the only thing he somehow carried with him, which was a receipt from the French gas and electricity company in Paris. It was the last document that we had. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must point out something that I forgot. While we were living on the mountains, physically it was very hard, the trip by itself and, and so forth, and then chopping wood to make a little bit of fire to cook things. My father developed a hernia. He was in such pain up on the mountains that it had to be operated immediately. So where were we going to have him operated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went down to the nearest village, and I spoke to a young lady I had gotten to know on the various trips from where we were down to that small village, very, very small. But they had a grocery store there. And occasionally, they would allow us a little bit of sugar, a little bit of oil to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had met this young lady. So I told her about the problems. She said, we'll take care of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my first [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] experience of, of the warmth of the Italian people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were total strangers to that-- no connection whatsoever. Mostly the people in that area looked at us with suspicion, the peasants in the area. We looked funny, we behaved funny, we were dressed funny. We were nothing like what they were used to. And what's worse, we were known to be Jews, the killers of Christ, and all that. Better stay away. Have nothing to do with them. She said, we'll take care. She and her father loaded my father, first to get him down from where we were in his excruciating pain down to her was quite a-- quite a-- quite a trip. Then they loaded him onto a cart and brought him-- I was not allowed to, to come along-- brought him to the nearest hospital, which was down in the valley De Monte, D-E-M-O-N-T-E, the town. And they came back in the evening, and they told us not to worry. They put him in under a fake name. They said he was a relative. He has been operated on. He's fine. And he will be let-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] released from the hospital shortly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now back to where I was, my father, and us on the train, with the wound barely bandaged. So the German officer comes to us. And my father shows him this ridiculous piece of identification. He said you have to come with me. So I knew this was the end. He took us to the end of the railroad car. And he saw that my father walked with great difficulty. He said, what's the matter? I said, well, he was just operated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went along with my father. My mother and brother remained seated in the compartment. He said, OK. In that case, let him sit here on the steps of the car going down to the rails. I'll be back shortly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I watched him go down the railroad tracks to some kind of command post. And for an agonizing 20, 25 minutes, my father said, look. They're probably going to take me. So you're in charge of the family now. You know our reference points. We had people we knew in Israel and some we knew here in case we could contact each other through these addresses.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see the officer coming back. And he says, you're in luck today. He said, we're not after bloodied Polish Jews. He says, we are after escaped Allied prisoners. OK. We got back on, on the train. As soon as it started moving, the entire compartment got up and applauded us, all the Italians. They came and embraced us. There was one lady there who had a basket of food covered with her clothes, a checkered cloth. She insisted on giving us all this food. I mention this because the contrasts of the average Italian citizen as compared to the French was shocking. We never encountered such empathy in, in France. Now mind you, I was steeped in the French tradition of liberte, egalite, fraternite. And France had a long democratic tradition. And the shock was to see how they turned around and collaborated with the Germans. I don't want to imply that all of them did. Of course, there was an underground, the Maquis, which you may have heard, that was very active. But the average French citizen never showed us such empathy, even those that we knew. Whereas here, suddenly out of nowhere these people, whom we hadn't exchanged two words with, who had only sensed the danger we were in without knowing what was happening-- was a marvelous experience, which I continued then to witness all throughout my stay in Italy. We made it to Florence and went immediately to the archdiocese, where we were met by the organizers. In charge of our group was a priest, [? Don Kazeeni ?], [? Don Leto Kazeeni ?], who immediately organized for the women to be hidden in a convent [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in a part of Florence called [? Vera ?] [? Longo. ?] So my mother and some other women that were in our group were hidden there. My brother, I, and my father, we were assigned temporarily into an abandoned movie house, abandoned because constructively, it wasn't up to-- it wasn't safe any, anymore. So we found ourselves there with, I would say, at least 100 men, temporarily sleeping there until they found us permanent hiding places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had the entire floor to ourselves. The benches, the rows of seats had been removed to the balcony. At the other end was a screen, and we had mattresses. We didn't stay there long, I would say maybe a week or 10 days, still waiting for permanent hiding places, during which time we went to visit my mother in the convent occasionally. We didn't stay in that abandoned cinema more than I would say a week or 10 days until the morning of November 6, 1943. It must have been about 5 o'clock in the morning, loud pounding on the door, continues, insisting. One of us got up out of his sleep in his pajama, walked to the main door. But before he could reach it, it burst open. And a flood of German and SS came, came in immediately surrounding all of us, and with barked orders to immediately gather our stuff, and go outside and load up into the trucks that were waiting. Some people were beaten, who tried to hide under the mattress. Others, because they were too slow in gathering their pathetic few things-- I helped my father, who wasn't very agile yet because his wound hadn't healed. And we were loaded into the trucks and brought to a place I didn't know, obviously. But it looked like a huge garrison. Because from-- through the top that covered our truck, I could see huge doors opening. And the truck moved in. The doors closed again. We were told immediately to disembark, to get off the trucks and assemble in front of two tables with German soldiers sitting there. And an officer explained that we have to surrender everything, money, jewels, cuff links, everything. And one by one, we approached the table. They took down our names, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the components of the family. And we were ordered into empty stables. I found out later that this used to be the headquarters of an Italian cavalry regiment, now abandoned, and taken over entirely by the Germans. There wasn't one Italian in, in, inside. So naturally, they had no use for horses. So they cleared the stables. And we were told to get in there and, and wait. As time passed during the day more and more Jews were being brought in. It turned out, again later-- something I didn't know at the time-- that this was a thoroughly methodically organized raid on all Jews of France-- of France-- of Florence. They knew where most of them were, were hidden, even in convents and other institutions. So I feared at any moment to see my mother walk, walk in. But it turned towards evening, and I didn't see my, my mother yet. In the evening, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a German soldier came and said, I need some volunteers to bring in some more straw. So I went with a friend of mine, same age, called Sigi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were brought by the German soldier to some kind of barn there where they had straw. We piled up the straw, as much as we could carry, and brought it back to the stables where we had been held. On the way, I noticed that the toilets which we were using, allowed to use-- and you couldn't just go to the toilet. You had to tell the German guard that you wanted to, to go. And he would wait until three, four, five, or six were ready to go. He would then bring you to the toilet, which was outside, wait for you to finish, and bring you back so that everyone was accounted for at all times. So going back and forth on two or three trips with the straw, I noticed that the toilets were very close to the outside wall. Now that, that wall was quite high. And on top were four rows of barbed wire. And the idea started taking root that perhaps I could escape. Preposterous as this may sound to you now, the idea of escaping from such a garrison, with such a high wall, surrounded entirely by Germans, was fantasy more than anything else. But it was prompted by something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Towards evening, at the time we were told to bring in the straw, we were given four bread rolls, in, in Italian called [ITALIAN], four bread rolls. And we were told to eat only two. So one of us, you know, asked, why only two? You need the other two in the morning for the train that will take you to Auschwitz. Now, at that time, of course, no one knew for sure what Auschwitz meant. We had heard the name. And how does one hear? Through rumors. I mean, nobody came and say, I just came from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we knew. We knew this was a place where you don't want to be. And that reinf--
